english: meal, flour, only in medical writings.
numer. figure: 95.

wylie: ce na, she na, zhe na
english: (cf. cìg (cig)), inst. of cès smrá-na (ces smra na), 'if one says so, asks so' etc., after words literally quoted, frq. (W. *zér-na* (*zer na*)).
jackal.

envious, jealous, mig cè-ré (mig ce re) (cèr-gyis (cer gyis) Thgr., cèr-te (cer te) Glr.) ltá-ba (lta ba) to look with an evil or envious eye upon; cè-ré loṅ-ba (ce re long ba) dim-sighted, purblind Cs.
bright, shining, of polished metal Glr., cf. krá-bo (kra bo).
scissors C.
1. a small sucking-pipe for drinking the Murva-beer, in which millet grains are swimming Sik. (v. Hook. I., 175). -- 2. a clyster-pipe.
v. cè-ré (ce re).

1. (Lex. इति), also śes (shes) and źes (zhes) (cf. cìg (cig)) so, thus, in ancient literature regularly placed after words or thoughts that are literally quoted, and so continuing the sentence; the quotation itself is gen. preceded by ,di skád-du ('di skad du), or ,di snyám-du ('di snyam du). In later literature cès (ces) and the introductory words are often omitted, in col. language always. Inst. of cès smrás-so (ces smras so), cès gsúṅs-so (ces gsungs so), so he said, thus he spoke, so has been said or spoken, so it is said, often only cès-so (ces so) is used, and in like manner cès-pa (ces pa) for cès smrás-pa (ces smras pa), this word, this speech; cès-pa-la sogs-pa (ces pa la sogs pa) these and similar words; cès-pa ,di yaṅ (ces pa 'di yang) also the preceding poem (is written by him); snyun (snyun) żès-pa (zhes pa) nád-kyi miṅ yin (nad kyi ming yin) the word snyun (snyun) is a term for 'disease' Zam.; żès(-pa) (zhes pa) daṅ (dang) 'such, and', if a quotation is followed by another, where we say 'further', 'moreover'; cès-pa-la (ces pa la) after words have been quoted, which form the subject of further discussion; cès byá-ba (ces bya ba), or cès-pa (ces pa) the so called, frq. after names; cès-su (ces su) rarely for cès (ces). -- 2. acc. to the usual spelling and pronunciation (cès (ces), cè (ce)) of the Lamas of Ld. it is the ordinary termination of the infinitive in W. (in Pur. and Bal. càs (cas), in Kun. cà (ca)), though etymologically as yet not accounted for; sometimes used also as a sbst. or adj. i.e. partic.: bsád-cès (bsad ces) killing, bsád-cès yin (bsad ces yin) it is to be killed; skyé-cès (skye ces) pregnant, v. skyé-ba (skye ba).
1. num. fig.: 125. -- 2. cò-dri-ba (co dri ba) Lex., C., to blame, reproach, slight; to vie with.
Mil. lark (not common in Tibet).
(?), cò-ger bżugs (co ger bzhugs) Glr., W. vulgo: *cò-gan dug* (*co gan dug*) he sits motionless.
also cò-ti (co ti), Cs.: a tuft of hair on the head, thus Lex.: cò-toi tor-còg (co to'i tor cog) (= cù-ti? (cu ti?)); cf. lcàṅ-lo (lcang lo).
= còr (cor), cáṅ cò-ri (cang co ri) Lex. (?).
= cù-li (cu li).
the prattling or chattering of little children Mil.; cf. cá-cò (ca co).

Cs.: a plural-sign; Schr. all (people). This, or a similar original meaning of the word is also to be traced in an expression usual in Ld.: cóg-mdo (cog mdo) a place where three roads meet, v. mdo (mdo); cf. also càg (cag). When affixed to a word, it must be preceded by the vowel o, the final consonant of the root being at the same time repeated. Affixed to verbs, it seems to convert them into participles: ,oṅs-so-còg-la ('ongs so cog la) Dzl. IV, 6, to those arrived, to the (persons) arrived, yin-no-còg (yin no cog), yód-do-còg (yod do cog) those being, existing (things or persons); Cs.: gcès-so-còg (gces so cog) things that are valuable, precious, to a man.
W. grasshopper, cricket.

C. to have leisure cóg-na yoṅ go (cog na yong go) if you have leisure, come! *cóg-ka* (*cog ka*) leisure, *dhe-riṅ cóg-ka me* (*de ring cog ka me*) to-day I have no leisure; *cóg-ka jhè* (*cog ka byed*) is an affirmative answer, when having been asked for some little service, something like: well, I'll do it.
